result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it on the tin roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to get in. Now the sky is just this pale, exhausted thing that forgets to cry.

I keep thinking about the moment before something breaks. Not the break itself. The breath held right before. Like the way my mother used to pause with the knife above the onion, knowing what came next would make her eyes water but doing it anyway. Some things are inevitable. We ritualize them to pretend we have control.

Yesterday I found a letter I wrote to myself at thirteen. The handwriting was furious, all sharp angles and underlined words. "Don't become boring," it said. "Don't become the kind of person who apologizes to furniture when they bump into it." I read it out loud in my empty kitchen and then I apologized to the counter for laughing too hard. The circle completes itself whether we want it to or not.

There's a bird that comes to my window every morning at 6:47. Always the same time. I don't know what kind it is. It has a red chest and it looks at me like it knows something I don't. Maybe it's the same bird every day. Maybe it's a hundred different birds taking turns to watch the sad man drink his coffee. Either way, we have an understanding. I don't open the window. It doesn't fly away.

I keep buying plants and they keep dying. Not dramatically. They just sort of... give up. One day they're green and reaching for something I can't see, the next they're brown and drooping like they've remembered a terrible secret. My apartment is becoming a graveyard of good intentions. I water the corpses anyway. It feels like church.

Sometimes I dance when no one's looking. Not the kind of dancing that has steps or grace. The kind where you become the music instead of following it. Last week I put on an old Prince record and moved like my skeleton was trying to escape my body. For three minutes and forty-seven seconds I wasn't thinking about rent or my father's cancer or the way my ex used to say my name like it was a question she was disappointed in the answer to. Then the song ended and I was just a man standing in his underwear surrounded by dead plants again.

The trick isn't to be happy. Anyone who tells you that is selling something. The trick is to be interested. In the way the light hits the edge of a glass at 4pm. In why certain songs make the hair on your arms stand up. In the fact that octopuses have three hearts and still choose to be solitary. There's a holiness in paying attention that church never taught me.

My neighbor is learning the violin. Badly. Every evening at 7:15 the sounds of tortured cats come through the wall. But sometimes, just sometimes, there's a note that's pure. A single clear tone that rises above the mess like a white flag. I find myself holding my breath waiting for it. Last night it lasted almost four seconds. I clapped when it was over. I don't know if she heard me.

I think we're all just trying to become the kind of people our younger selves wouldn't be embarrassed by. That's the real work. Not success or love or any of the shiny things we chase. Just trying not to let the world make us small in all the ways it wants to. The world is very good at making things small.

My grandfather used to say that every man needs three things: a good pair of boots, a song he can sing when he's alone, and someone who'll miss him when he's gone. I have the boots. The song changes every year but it always finds me. The third one... I'm working on it.

The rain really doesn't fall here anymore. But sometimes at night I can smell it coming. Like the sky is remembering how to be generous. I leave my window open just in case. The bird doesn't come at night. Just the possibility of rain and the sound of my neighbor's violin wrapping itself around my ribs like a promise.

Tomorrow I'll buy another plant. Maybe this one will live. Maybe it won't. Either way, I'll talk to it. Tell it about the bird and the letter and the way Prince makes my bones want to leave my body. Plants like when you talk to them. Even the dead ones. Especially the dead ones.

The world is so loud with its certainty. But underneath all that noise is this other thing. This quiet music that only plays when you stop trying to conduct it. I don't have the words for it. No one does. That's why we have birds and violins and thirteen-year-old boys who write angry letters to their future selves.

That's why we have 1000 words that somehow aren't enough and somehow say everything anyway.

The rain is coming. I can feel it in my teeth.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
